core_intuitions: Every benchmark number is a random variable with sampling error; the 95% margin near p=0.5 is about 1/sqrt(n), so it shrinks only as 1/sqrt(n) and small gaps demand huge test sets.
Overlapping confidence intervals mean 'no demonstrated gap' — rank order without interval separation is noise, and this is the single leaderboard-reading skill the chapter exists to instill.

Pairing crushes variance: evaluate A and B on the SAME items and only the discordant items (one right, one wrong) carry comparative signal — concordant agreements are thrown away, which is exactly what McNemar formalizes.

The bootstrap builds a sampling distribution empirically by resampling your own data with replacement and recomputing the statistic many times; the 2.5/97.5 percentiles of that cloud are the CI, with no distributional assumptions.

Noise has several independent sources (items, prompts, seeds, judges) each divided by a different denominator; a single-prompt eval divides prompt variance by 1, so prompt noise is invisible to an item-only CI and often dominates.
An item's information about a model peaks when the item's difficulty matches the model's ability, which is why adaptive testing reaches fixed-test precision with a fraction of the items.

new_figures.name: statrig-ci-overlap-verdict
new_figures.concept: A leaderboard read with error bars: overlapping intervals mean 'no demonstrated gap', separated intervals mean a real difference.
new_figures.rationale: This is the thesis the chapter explicitly exists to instill (stated in the intro and the closing 'Reading Leaderboards' section). A reader who internalizes one image from this chapter should internalize this one: point-estimate rank is meaningless until intervals separate. No other figure carries the chapter's headline skill.
new_figures.brief: A horizontal leaderboard-style panel. Left: a vertical list of 4-5 model rows (label them Model A, B, C, D generically, never real names). Each row is a horizontal error-bar: a dot for the point estimate with a symmetric whisker for the 95% CI, plotted against a shared horizontal accuracy axis (show a generic axis, e.g. tick marks without inventing benchmark numbers — use relative positions only). Arrange so the TOP TWO models (A and B) have heavily OVERLAPPING intervals even though A's dot sits slightly to the right — annotate this pair with a bracket and the verdict 'CIs overlap -> NO demonstrated gap; rank between A and B is noise'. Arrange the THIRD model (C) so its interval sits clearly to the LEFT with NO overlap with A/B — annotate 'separated -> real difference'. Add a small caption strip reminding: 'width ~ 1/sqrt(n); to halve it, 4x the items'. Optionally show one row (a 'new entrant', Model D) with a much WIDER interval labelled 'few evaluations -> rank not yet meaningful'. Theme-safe: use the theme's neutral/foreground for bars and a single accent hue only for the verdict brackets; no hardcoded red/green. Everything must fit; keep whisker labels from colliding. The static frame alone must make the overlap-vs-separation contrast legible at a glance.

new_figures.name: statrig-pairing-discordant-cells
new_figures.concept: A 2x2 contingency of A-correct vs B-correct: only the two off-diagonal (discordant) cells carry signal about which model is better; the diagonal agreements are discarded.
new_figures.rationale: Pairing is the chapter's most powerful practical trick ('The Question You Actually Care About') and the McNemar mechanic is genuinely counter-intuitive — that you THROW AWAY the items both models agree on. A single 2x2 makes 'why pairing crushes variance' click far better than the covariance algebra: the huge agreeing cells are exactly the item-difficulty variance that pairing cancels.
new_figures.placement: Three paired tests and when to use each
new_figures.brief: A 2x2 grid. Columns = 'B correct' / 'B wrong'; rows = 'A correct' / 'A wrong'. Fill the two DIAGONAL cells (both-right top-left, both-wrong bottom-right) with a muted/greyed treatment and label them 'concordant — carry NO comparative signal, discarded'. Use small illustrative integers for cell counts consistent with the chapter's worked example (both-right 372, both-wrong 38, A-right-only b=51, B-right-only c=33) so it ties directly to the text — these are the text's own numbers, not invented. Highlight the two OFF-diagonal cells: top-right 'A right, B wrong = b (=51)' and bottom-left 'A wrong, B right = c (=33)' with the accent hue, labelled 'discordant — the ONLY items that vote'. Off to the side, show the McNemar intuition as a tiny coin: 'under H0, each of the b+c=84 discordant items is a fair coin; is 51 vs 33 more lopsided than chance?'. Include a one-line takeaway strip: 'pairing cancels the difficulty variance living in the diagonal — worth a 4-10x larger unpaired test set.' Keep the grid square and uncluttered; ensure the greyed diagonal still reads clearly in dark theme (don't let it vanish). Theme-safe: single accent hue for discordant cells, neutral foreground elsewhere.

new_figures.name: statrig-bootstrap-resample
new_figures.concept: The bootstrap: resample the scored items with replacement, recompute the statistic, repeat thousands of times, and read the 2.5/97.5 percentiles of the resulting cloud as the CI.
new_figures.rationale: The bootstrap is the mechanical heart of the whole CI section and the one idea that generalizes to Elo, F1, percentiles — 'any statistic'. Its intuition is inherently a repeated process: the sampling distribution is BUILT UP by accumulation. Motion shows the histogram forming from nothing, which a single frame states but does not let you feel. This is a legitimate use of animation.
new_figures.brief: Three-stage left-to-right pipeline that also works as a single static frame. STAGE 1 (left): the original sample — a small row of n scored items drawn as tiles (some marked pass, some fail, small n like 12 for legibility). STAGE 2 (middle): a 'resample with replacement' operation — show a new row drawn from the original where SOME tiles are duplicated and some are MISSING (make duplication visually obvious, e.g. two copies of the same tile), with a small die/arrow icon labelled 'draw n indices with replacement'. Compute one statistic value (the resample mean) shown as a single dot dropping onto a histogram axis on the right. STAGE 3 (right): a histogram of accumulated resample statistics building up; mark the central point estimate and shade the middle 95%, with the 2.5th and 97.5th percentile edges labelled 'lo' and 'hi' and a bracket 'percentile bootstrap 95% CI'. ANIMATION: loop the resample->recompute->drop-a-dot cycle so the histogram visibly grows from a few dots to a full bell, then the percentile bracket snaps in; a small counter 'replicate b = 1 ... B'. The FINAL/static frame must show the completed histogram with the CI bracket so a still reader gets the whole idea. Small caption: 'no distributional assumptions — works for accuracy, Elo, F1, p90 latency.' Theme-safe: neutral tiles with pass/fail distinguished by shape or fill pattern (not red/green), single accent hue for the shaded 95% band; ensure duplicated tiles are distinguishable in both themes. Keep n small and labels non-overlapping.

new_figures.name: statrig-variance-budget-denominators
new_figures.concept: The reported-mean variance is a sum of components (item, prompt, seed, judge) each divided by its OWN sample count; a single prompt divides prompt variance by 1, so it never shrinks and often dominates.
new_figures.rationale: The variance-decomposition section's key, non-obvious lesson lives entirely in the denominators of one equation: extra items do nothing for prompt variance. This trips up practitioners constantly ('we changed the system prompt and the score moved 4 points'). A budget/bar visual of the terms makes visible that prompt noise is invisible to an item-only CI — a purely verbal treatment does not land.
new_figures.brief: A 'variance budget' diagram of the reported-mean variance as a stack/sum of blocks, one per source: item, prompt, seed, judge, residual. Render each source as a block whose area/height suggests its contribution, and CRUCIALLY annotate each block with its denominator from the chapter's formula: item / n_i, prompt / n_t, seed / (n_i*n_s), judge / n_j, residual / (n_i*n_s*n_j). Show TWO scenarios side by side to make the lesson pop: LEFT 'single prompt (n_t = 1)' where the prompt block is large because it is divided by 1 and is shaded with the accent hue and labelled 'invisible to an item-only CI'; RIGHT 'n_t = 3 prompts' where the same prompt block has shrunk to a third. In both, show the item block already small because n_i is large, with an arrow 'more items shrinks THIS block only'. Add a takeaway strip: 'You cannot fix prompt variance by buying more items — vary the prompt.' Use small illustrative integers only (n_i large like 500, n_t = 1 vs 3, n_s = 3), invent no scores. Theme-safe: neutral blocks, single accent hue for the prompt block being highlighted; keep denominators legible and non-overlapping in both themes.

new_figures.name: statrig-irt-item-information
new_figures.concept: An item's Fisher information about a model peaks where the item's difficulty equals the model's ability; high-discrimination items give a tall narrow information peak, and adaptive testing serves items near the model's ability frontier.
new_figures.rationale: IRT and adaptive evaluation are a whole section built on one geometric fact: the information curve I(theta) = a^2 P(1-P) peaks at P=0.5, i.e. difficulty = ability. Visualizing the item-characteristic sigmoid paired with its bell-shaped information curve makes 'why ask items near the model's ability' obvious, and contrasting high vs low discrimination shows which items are worth their weight — the engine behind tinyBenchmarks-style 10-100x savings.
new_figures.brief: A two-panel figure sharing a horizontal ability axis theta. TOP panel: item characteristic curves — plot P(correct) = sigma(a(theta - b)) as an S-curve, with a vertical dashed line at theta = b marking 'difficulty: 50% pass point'. Overlay TWO items: a HIGH-discrimination item (steep sigmoid, large a) and a LOW-discrimination item (shallow sigmoid, small a) so the reader sees discrimination = steepness. BOTTOM panel, aligned beneath: the corresponding Fisher information curves I(theta) = a^2 P(1-P) — bell-shaped, each PEAKING exactly at its own theta = b; the high-a item's bell is TALL and NARROW ('cleanly separates strong from weak models'), the low-a item's bell is short and broad ('everyone guesses -> noise'). Mark a model's current ability estimate theta_hat as a movable marker on the axis and annotate 'adaptive testing picks the item whose peak sits at theta_hat -> maximal info per question -> fixed-test precision in a fraction of the items'. Keep curves schematic (no invented numeric scores; axes labelled qualitatively low->high). Theme-safe: two distinguishable line styles/hues for the two items that work in both themes (e.g. solid vs dashed plus an accent hue), gridlines subtle, labels non-overlapping, both panels inside the viewBox with the shared axis clearly aligned.

summary: This concept-rich chapter currently has ZERO figures despite covering six distinct statistical pillars, so it is figure-poor and warrants a focused foundational set rather than zero. I propose five, strictly ranked. Three P1 must-haves carry the chapter's core teachable ideas: (1) an error-bar leaderboard showing overlapping-vs-separated CIs — the single leaderboard-reading skill the chapter explicitly exists to instill; (2) the McNemar 2x2 contingency showing that only discordant cells vote, which makes 'why pairing crushes variance' click; and (3) a bootstrap resample->recompute->accumulate animation, the mechanical heart of the CI section and the only figure where motion (a histogram building up into a sampling distribution) genuinely earns a long-animation. Two P2/P3 figures cover the remaining big ideas that are hard to convey verbally: a variance-budget diagram whose denominators reveal that prompt noise is invisible to an item-only CI, and the IRT item-information curves showing information peaks where difficulty meets ability. All briefs use only the chapter's own illustrative integers (e.g. the worked-example McNemar cells 372/38/51/33), invent no benchmark numbers, and are specified to be fully understandable as static frames in both themes.
